description: A rare Southern California oak whose canopy and leaf litter anchor a massive insect base—caterpillars in particular—that feed nesting songbirds in Doug Tallamy’s food-web framing. Acorns extend support into fall for jays, woodpeckers, and mammals.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Deep soils on slopes and mesas; avoid compacted fill over root zone
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained loam to clay loam; tolerates seasonal dryness; pH adaptable

maintenance.wateringNotes: Deep soak occasionally during the first 2–3 years; once established, avoid summer irrigation near the trunk to reduce Phytophthora risk.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove dead, damaged, or rubbing limbs; avoid heavy crown thinning
maintenance.pruningNotes: Prune during warm dry weather when pathogens are less active; never flush-cut large limbs against the trunk without guidance from a certified arborist.
maintenance.specialNotes: Maintain a mulch-free collar a few inches from the bark; keep irrigation emitters outside the dripline once mature.

seed.description: Acorns mature in fall—critical calories for scrub-jays, woodpeckers, and small mammals

phenology.ecologicalValue: Oak-rich insect communities (especially caterpillars) drive chickadee and titmouse productivity; acorns propagate woodland patches used by raptors and mammals.

wildlife.notes: Larvae skeletonize new foliage; boom years pulse caterpillar biomass for insectivorous birds.
Caterpillars use oaks in coastal and foothill woodlands; adults sip mud and rotting fruit nearby.
Large silk moth larvae feed on oak leaves before pupating underground.
Stores acorns in granary trees; links oak mast to year-round woodpecker colonies.

description: An evergreen oak whose foliage supports a deep bench of native caterpillars—the protein engine for spring nesting birds—while acorns stitch together mammal and corvid food webs through winter.

plantingRequirements.sunExposure: Full Sun to Partial Shade (seedlings appreciate afternoon shade)
plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Hillsides, ravine sides, and terrace sites with room for lateral roots
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Deep, well-drained loam preferred; tolerates clay if drainage is adequate; pH roughly 4–8

maintenance.wateringNotes: Establish with deep, infrequent water; mature trees should receive at most occasional summer soak and never frequent lawn sprinklers.
maintenance.pruningTask: Clean out deadwood and improve clearance for structures
maintenance.pruningNotes: Calscape recommends dry-season pruning to limit pathogen entry; avoid lion-tailing interior foliage.
maintenance.specialNotes: Underplant with smaller natives to shade roots; keep irrigation heads away from the trunk.

seed.description: Acorns ripen in autumn; mast years flood the landscape with calories for birds and mammals

phenology.ecologicalValue: Documented host for dozens of butterfly and moth species; acorns sustain jays, squirrels, and deer while cavities shelter owls and woodpeckers.

wildlife.notes: Periodic outbreaks dramatically increase caterpillar availability along the coast.
Larvae feed on young oak leaves; adults patrol sunny openings.
Probes leaf litter under oaks for insects and uses dense canopy for cover.
Caches acorns across the landscape, unintentionally planting the next generation of oaks.

description: Riparian willows stitch stream corridors into wildlife highways—early pollen and soft leaves feed specialist bees, leaf-mining insects, and butterflies whose caterpillars fuel bird productivity.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Seasonally moist swales, arroyo margins, and low spots; tolerates bank erosion
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy loam to silty clay; tolerates seasonal inundation; neutral to slightly acidic typical of riparian soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: Outside natural channels, provide deep weekly water the first year, tapering to twice monthly in dry summers once roots reach moisture.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove storm-damaged stems and raise canopy for sight lines
maintenance.pruningNotes: Prune during winter dormancy to minimize sap loss and encourage vigorous spring flush.
maintenance.specialNotes: Expect vigorous suckering; clip root sprouts if a single-trunk form is desired.

seed.description: Cottony wind-dispersed seeds release after catkins mature—important early cover for riparian insects

phenology.ecologicalValue: Catkins feed early bees; foliage hosts admiral and swallowtail larvae; dense stems shelter songbirds and dragonflies over water.

wildlife.notes: Caterpillars roll willow leaves; adults defend sunny perches along streams.
Larvae mimic bird droppings on willow twigs before pupating on silk mats.
Queens and workers mob willow catkins for protein-rich pollen after winter.
Weaves pendulous nests in outer willow branches over moving water.

description: Evergreen backbone of coastal sage scrub and chaparral edges, toyon couples late nectar with winter berry crops that carry birds through lean months in Tallamy’s emphasis on sequential resources.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Dry slopes, terraces, and raised berms with excellent drainage
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Lean, well-drained sandy loam to rocky clay; tolerates droughty coastal soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: Monthly deep soak the first summer after planting; withhold irrigation once roots are established to preserve compact form.
maintenance.pruningTask: Thin interior stems and shorten long leaders after the heaviest berry drop
maintenance.pruningNotes: Late-winter shaping keeps fruiting wood productive without removing developing flower buds.
maintenance.specialNotes: Avoid shearing into tight cubes—open habit supports more birds and better air flow.

berry.description: Bright pomes ripen through winter, feeding thrushes, waxwings, and mammals

phenology.ecologicalValue: Dense foliage shelters quail and wrens; berries bridge the nutritional gap between fall insect decline and spring wildflowers.

wildlife.notes: Sips tiny white flowers along outer branch tips in early summer.
Winter visitor that focuses on persistent red fruits in scrub and backyard patches.
Forms noisy flocks that strip toyon clusters during cold snaps.
Ground-foraging bird that reaches lower branches for fruit and gleans insects in leaf litter below.

description: Fast-growing deciduous shrub whose flat flower clusters become pollinator airports in spring, then blue berries fuel birds and small mammals—classic Tallamy-style stacked resources in one plant.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Moist canyon bottoms, north slopes, or irrigated wildlife hedgerows
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Rich loam with steady moisture; tolerates clay if drainage is moderate

maintenance.wateringNotes: Keep root zone evenly moist while flowering and fruiting; taper water after berries drop unless natural subsurface moisture exists.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove one-third of the oldest canes at the base after fruiting
maintenance.pruningNotes: Elderberries bloom on new wood; rotational cane removal keeps plants productive and reduces powdery mildew buildup.
maintenance.specialNotes: Cook berries before human consumption; leave ample fruit for wildlife.

berry.description: Waxy blue drupes ripen in early summer—high-sugar food for songbirds and fruit bats

phenology.ecologicalValue: Spring umbels aggregate hundreds of bee visits per hour; fruiting heads extend support into songbird migration.

wildlife.notes: Works the margins of inflorescences for nectar and small insects.
Major pollen collector on flat-topped elder inflorescences.
Crushes soft fruits with heavy bill; often visits shrubs at woodland edges.
Forms loose flocks that clean berry clusters in a single afternoon.

description: A dioecious aster-family workhorse whose late-season nectar extends pollinator forage when many plants are dormant, amplifying insect energy available to insectivorous birds.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Dry banks, roadsides, and coarse alluvium; tolerates reflected heat
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy, rocky, or lean clay; highly drought tolerant once established

maintenance.wateringNotes: No supplemental irrigation needed in Poway once established; overwatering causes lanky weak stems.
maintenance.pruningTask: Cut back rangy stems by half to encourage dense flowering wood
maintenance.pruningNotes: Rejuvenate before spring growth resumes; wear gloves—sap can irritate skin.
maintenance.specialNotes: Plant both male and female clones if you want maximum seed for finches; single-sex plantings still support pollinators.

seed.description: Fluffy pappus carries achenes on wind—finches pick at clusters for oil-rich seeds

phenology.ecologicalValue: Late nectar supports migrating monarchs and resident bees; seeds attract goldfinches and sparrows after bloom.

wildlife.notes: Workers tank up on autumn nectar before winter dormancy.
Large bees rob nectar through short-tubed disc flowers at dusk and dawn.
Specialist long-horned females pack Baccharis pollen for late-season brood cells.
Balances seed diet with insects in spring but shifts to composite seeds in fall.

description: Aromatic evergreen sumac builds quick cover along fire-prone slopes; while less of a caterpillar powerhouse than oaks, it still threads nectar, fruit, and shelter through coastal sage scrub food webs.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Warm south- and west-facing slopes; tolerates wind exposure
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained sandy to rocky soils; intolerant of prolonged wet feet

maintenance.wateringNotes: Natural rainfall suffices; supplemental water only the first dry season after planting.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove frost-nipped tips and thin crowded interior stems
maintenance.pruningNotes: Minimal pruning preserves the iconic 'tent' silhouette and reduces entry points for pathogens.
maintenance.specialNotes: Volatile oils make it flammable—situate with defensible space in wildfire-prone lots.

berry.description: Sticky white drupes ripen before midsummer—attractive to finches and fruit-eating mammals

phenology.ecologicalValue: Early-season nectar pairs with dense evergreen cover used by wrens and towhees escaping predators.

wildlife.notes: Visits frothy flower panicles at scrub edges.
Workers collect both nectar and light pollen loads from small flowers.
Tests sticky fruits and caches viable seeds in scrub openings.
Uses dense interior branches for nesting and predator escape year-round.

description: The dominant manzanita of San Diego County's coastal sage scrub and chaparral — the 8th most observed native plant in the Poway area. Dense evergreen canopy provides year-round bird cover, while early winter blooms are critical nectar for overwintering hummingbirds and native bees when almost nothing else flowers. Berries feed thrushes and small mammals through summer.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Slopes and hillsides on fast-draining soils; excellent on south-facing chaparral exposures
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained sandy or decomposed granite soils; intolerant of summer irrigation near the crown

maintenance.wateringNotes: No irrigation after establishment — summer water causes root rot. If needed the first year, soak deeply only during the cool season (November–March).
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove dead wood and lightly shape after bloom; thin crossing branches to improve air circulation
maintenance.pruningNotes: Avoid heavy shearing — manzanitas compartmentalize wounds slowly. Remove only clearly dead material.
maintenance.specialNotes: Maintain a gravel or bare-soil zone around the crown base. Do not pile organic mulch against stems — it promotes Phytophthora.

berry.description: Mealy berry-like drupes ripen through late spring and summer, eaten by thrushes, mockingbirds, and small mammals

phenology.ecologicalValue: Critical winter nectar source when almost nothing else blooms; dense canopy shelters nesting birds year-round; berries extend fruit resources through the dry season.

wildlife.notes: Territorial males defend winter-blooming manzanita clusters — one of the few nectar sources during the cool season.
Larvae feed on manzanita foliage and flower buds; adults emerge to nectar on the same shrubs.
Uses its curved bill to probe among manzanita branches for ripe berries and ground-dwelling invertebrates in the leaf litter below.
Dense manzanita canopy provides year-round nesting and roosting habitat for this chaparral obligate.

description: A coastal sage scrub keystone whose long-lived inflorescences shift from cream to rust, offering nectar, pollen, and seed in sequence so generalist and specialist pollinators rarely hit a resource gap.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Dry slopes, berms, and raised planters with sharp drainage
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to gravelly loam; pH 5–8.5; intolerant of soggy clay bowls

maintenance.wateringNotes: Withhold summer water entirely once established; occasional deep soak only if foliage shrivels during extreme drought.
maintenance.pruningTask: Shear off last year's spent flower heads to reveal fresh gray foliage
maintenance.pruningNotes: Light haircut before spring growth removes thatch and discourages stem borers.
maintenance.specialNotes: Leave some rust-colored heads standing—they hold seeds for finches well into winter.

seed.description: Small angled achenes mature inside persistent rusty involucres—finch candy

phenology.ecologicalValue: Documented host for metalmark butterflies; seed heads extend forage after nectar peaks.

wildlife.notes: Caterpillars tie buckwheat heads with silk; adults nectar at nearby composites.
Uses coastal buckwheat species as primary host plants in scrub habitats.
Federal candidate species that relies on long-season buckwheat patches in San Diego County.
Balances insect protein with oil-rich buckwheat seeds in late summer.

description: Aromatic salvia flowers pack high-sugar nectar into the heart of coastal sage scrub bloom, stitching hummingbirds, specialist bees, and day-flying moths into the same aromatic network Tallamy highlights for habitat connectivity.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Dry slopes, rock gardens, and berms with air movement
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Lean, fast-draining sandy or rocky soils; tolerates mildly alkaline pH typical of inland coastal scrub

maintenance.wateringNotes: No summer irrigation once established; overwatering promotes root pathogens and floppy growth.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove spent flowering spikes and lightly tip-branch to maintain compact habit
maintenance.pruningNotes: Post-bloom haircut keeps plants bushy without cutting into old hardwood.
maintenance.specialNotes: Fragrant foliage repels deer; combine with buckwheat for staggered bloom.

seed.description: Nutlets mature after corollas drop—finches and sparrows glean them from dry spikes

phenology.ecologicalValue: High nectar value supports migrant hummingbirds and bumblebees; aromatic oils reduce herbivory pressure on neighboring plants.

wildlife.notes: Male display dives focus on tall sage spikes in coastal scrub.
Territorial feeding on early-season whorled flowers before chaparral peaks.
Workers perform buzz-pollination on bilabiate sage corollas.
Crepuscular hovering visits load migrating moths with sugar before desert flights.

description: Silver-leaved coastal sage scrub icon whose tall spikes of white flowers concentrate nectar and pollen during a mid-spring pulse, concentrating bee and hummingbird traffic when Tallamy-style food webs are ramping caterpillar loads on nearby woody plants.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Raised berms, dry slopes, and coarse soils with excellent air drainage
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Lean, well-drained sandy to rocky loam; intolerant of summer wet feet and heavy organic mulch against the crown

maintenance.wateringNotes: No supplemental summer irrigation once established; a few deep soaks November–March only the first year after planting.
maintenance.pruningTask: Remove spent flowering wands and tip-prune rangy stems after seeds finish maturing
maintenance.pruningNotes: Late-fall shaping keeps a compact silhouette without cutting into old hardwood that resprouts poorly.
maintenance.specialNotes: Fragrant foliage is highly deer-resistant; avoid overhead sprinklers that keep foliage wet.

seed.description: Four nutlets mature in drying calyces—finches and sparrows glean them after corollas fall

phenology.ecologicalValue: High-sugar nectar supports migrating hummingbirds and long-tongued bees; aromatic oils reduce mammal browsing pressure on neighboring seedlings.

wildlife.notes: Territorial feeding on vertical spikes of white flowers in warm inland canyons.
Desert-edge males track early sage blooms when inland heat builds.
Workers buzz-pollinate bilabiate sage flowers, packing pollen for late-spring brood.
Crepuscular hovering visits load migrating moths on tall nectar wands.

description: Aromatic foundation shrub of coastal sage scrub whose wind-pollinated flowers still anchor late-season insect communities while dense twigs shelter ground-foraging birds—classic structure-and-chemistry habitat that Tallamy links to persistent resident bird productivity.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Dry slopes, coastal bluffs, and berms with continuous air movement
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to rocky loam; tolerates mildly alkaline inland soils; avoid rich compost that favors rank, short-lived growth

maintenance.wateringNotes: Natural rainfall suffices in Poway; summer irrigation promotes root rots and lanky stems.
maintenance.pruningTask: Lightly thin dead twigs and shorten leggy leaders before winter rains
maintenance.pruningNotes: Minimal cuts preserve the natural mound; avoid shearing into tight balls that sun-scald interior wood.
maintenance.specialNotes: Volatile oils are flammable—maintain defensible space on fire-prone lots.

seed.description: Tiny wind-dispersed achenes release after disc flowers mature—finches pick at spent heads

phenology.ecologicalValue: Specialist leaf beetles recycle foliage biomass; seeds extend finch forage after spring wildflowers fade.

wildlife.notes: Chrysomelid adults and larvae clip foliage, exporting nitrogen to insectivorous birds via the arthropod ladder.
Workers still visit inconspicuous autumn flowers for trace nectar and pollen.
Balances insect protein in spring with composite-family seeds as heads dry down.
Gleans leaf litter and twig crevices under sage mounds for spiders and beetles year-round.

description: Fast-sprouting sunflower-family shrub whose bright yellow heads export pollen and nectar early in the dry season, helping generalist pollinators bridge the gap between winter rains and peak buckwheat bloom in Tallamy’s calendar of stacked resources.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Warm coastal and inland slopes; tolerates wind and salt spray near scrub edges
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained sandy loam to rocky clay; tolerates droughty, low-organic soils typical of sage scrub

maintenance.wateringNotes: Withhold irrigation after establishment; occasional deep soak only if foliage wilts during extreme drought.
maintenance.pruningTask: Cut back rangy stems by one-third after heaviest seed shed
maintenance.pruningNotes: Late-winter rejuvenation encourages dense flowering wood before spring growth resumes.
maintenance.specialNotes: Self-sows freely on bare mineral soil—ideal for erosion blankets but less ideal next to irrigated turf.

seed.description: Single-seeded achenes crowned with sparse pappus—goldfinches and sparrows work dry heads

phenology.ecologicalValue: Composite inflorescences aggregate long-horned bees; foliage supports a specialist leaf-beetle guild that fuels insectivorous birds.

wildlife.notes: Leaf skeletonizers convert foliage into beetle biomass consumed by bushtits and flycatchers.
Migratory waves tank up on yellow disc flowers before inland desert flights.
Females pack composite pollen on scopal hairs for underground brood cells.
Strips drying heads for oil-rich sunflower achenes in late summer.

description: Sticky-flowered sage scrub shrub whose tubular red blooms favor long-tongued pollinators, tightening mutualisms between hummingbirds, large bees, and checkerspot caterpillars in the same Tallamy-style plant–insect coupling seen across monkeyflower relatives.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Rocky banks, north- and east-facing scrub edges, and raised planters with sharp drainage
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Coarse sandy loam to decomposed granite; keep crowns dry in summer; avoid heavy clay bowls

maintenance.wateringNotes: Deep soak monthly the first dry season only; established plants decline with summer lawn irrigation.
maintenance.pruningTask: Deadhead spent spikes and lightly thin crowded basal stems after peak bloom
maintenance.pruningNotes: Avoid cutting into old wood without live buds; post-bloom shaping maintains air flow around sticky foliage.
maintenance.specialNotes: Hummingbird magnet near patios; expect some aphid honeydew that supports bushtit and ant interactions.

seed.description: Capsules split to release dust-like seeds once pods brown—natural reseeding in gravelly gaps

phenology.ecologicalValue: Nectar tube morphology filters pollinator cast; foliage hosts checkerspot larvae that move bird energy up the food web.

wildlife.notes: Larvae chew sticky leaves before pupating on nearby stems; adults nectar at adjacent composites.
Repeated feeding bouts on red tubular corollas at scrub edges.
Males incorporate tall monkeyflower spikes into late-spring display territories.
Large workers rob nectar legitimately at the corolla mouth, transferring sticky pollen loads.

description: A milkweed whose narrow leaves and umbels concentrate cardenolides, making it a linchpin host for monarch and queen larvae while still pumping nectar to dozens of bees and wasps—an exemplar of Tallamy’s specialist-host plants that disproportionately support arthropod diversity.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Swales, meadow pockets, and gentle slopes where winter moisture lingers but crowns dry by summer
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy loam to silty clay; tolerates seasonal saturation if surface dries between events

maintenance.wateringNotes: Deep soak twice monthly through the first summer, then taper to natural rainfall unless foliage wilts during heat waves.
maintenance.pruningTask: Cut dormant stems near the base once pods have opened and monarchs have eclosed
maintenance.pruningNotes: Delay cutting until late winter so hollow stalks shelter overwintering beneficial insects.
maintenance.specialNotes: Expect aphids—they support syrphids and lacewings that feed insectivorous birds.

seed.description: Follicles split to release silk-borne seeds—critical handoff to wind and goldfinches

phenology.ecologicalValue: Umbels aggregate diverse pollinators; milky latex supports a recognizable milkweed insect guild that ripples up to swallows and flycatchers.

wildlife.notes: Females oviposit on fresh whorled leaves; larvae sequester cardenolides that advertise toxicity to birds.
Southern monarch relative whose caterpillars share milkweed hosts in warmer inland valleys.
Cerambycid adults chew flowers and leaves, recycling milkweed tissue into beetle biomass.
Brightly aposematic seed feeders aggregate on drying follicles after bloom.

description: Summer-dry subshrub whose scarlet tubes open when many natives pause bloom, extending hummingbird and sphinx-moth energy into the hottest months—precisely the kind of seasonal redundancy Tallamy emphasizes for resilient food webs.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Rocky banks, wall tops, and berms with fast drainage; afternoon shade acceptable inland
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Lean, gritty loam; tolerates decomposed granite; intolerant of soggy heavy clay

maintenance.wateringNotes: Optional monthly soak July–August only if foliage shrivels; otherwise mimic dry-summer foothill regimes.
maintenance.pruningTask: Cut stems to basal woody framework after frost browns foliage
maintenance.pruningNotes: Hard winter haircut rejuvenates flowering wood without summer pathogens entering cuts.
maintenance.specialNotes: Spreads by underground rhizomes—useful for stabilizing slopes but give it room from delicate neighbors.

seed.description: Elongate capsules split to release plumed seeds on autumn winds

phenology.ecologicalValue: Late nectar supports migrating hummingbirds; tubular morphology favors long-tongued pollinators over generalist flies.

wildlife.notes: Repeated visits to scarlet tubes during midday heat when other natives pause flowering.
Migrants and breeders work long corollas along riparian and scrub ecotones.
Hovering crepuscular visits align with tubular evening-primrose relatives.
Workers rob nectar at the corolla base, transferring pollen on sternal scopa.

description: Aromatic everlasting mats throw flat yellow heads that behave like airport hubs for small bees and butterflies, while foliage hosts checkerspot and lady relatives—low stature but high leverage in Tallamy’s ground-layer insect economy.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Dry slopes, rock gardens, and trail edges with sharp drainage
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to rocky loam; tolerates low-nutrient soils; avoid wet winter pockets

maintenance.wateringNotes: No irrigation after establishment; mis-aimed drip emitters that keep crowns wet invite crown rot.
maintenance.pruningTask: Shear spent flower heads to neat mounds after seed drop slows
maintenance.pruningNotes: Mid-summer deadheading keeps mats compact and discourages stem borers.
maintenance.specialNotes: Leave a few dried heads if you want volunteer seedlings in gravelly gaps.

seed.description: Achenes crowned with short pappus release from brown disc heads—finch forage

phenology.ecologicalValue: Sunflower-family architecture aggregates small native bees; hairy leaves reduce rabbit pressure on neighboring forbs.

wildlife.notes: Caterpillars tie leaf nests in everlasting mats; adults sip nectar from the same heads.
Migrants tank up on flat yellow disc flowers before continuing inland.
Long-horned females work shallow disc flowers in warm morning sun.
Strips drying heads for small oily achenes after bloom.

description: Coastal scrub sunflower with resinous leaves and long-season yellow blooms that keeps small bees and flower flies on station, exporting pollen energy to insectivorous birds long after spring annuals finish in Tallamy’s phenological framing.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Dry terraces, wall crowns, and south-facing scrub banks
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Well-drained sandy loam to rocky clay; tolerates coastal alkalinity

maintenance.wateringNotes: Natural rainfall suffices; first-year plants accept occasional deep soak March–May only.
maintenance.pruningTask: Tip-prune winter-killed tips and shorten last season’s flowering stems
maintenance.pruningNotes: Light spring shaping opens the crown before new growth obscures cuts.
maintenance.specialNotes: Resinous foliage is deer-resistant but can spot pavement with sticky drip after heavy aphid years.

seed.description: Achenes mature in brown involucres—goldfinches and sparrows glean late-summer heads

phenology.ecologicalValue: Composite heads extend pollinator forage into early summer; seeds support granivorous songbirds.

wildlife.notes: Nectars at yellow disc flowers during spring migration pulses.
Females collect sunflower-type pollen for subterranean nests.
Balances insect diet with oil-rich achenes as heads dry.
Communal ground-nesting sweat bees work shallow disc flowers in warm weather.

description: Nitrogen-fixing subshrub that greens up scrub after fire, converting atmospheric nitrogen into protein-rich foliage browsed by insects and then by insectivorous birds—an underappreciated Tallamy-style connector between soil fertility and arthropod biomass.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Disturbed cuts, road verges, and dry slopes where roots can run deep
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to rocky soils; tolerates serpentine margins; intolerant of prolonged shade and summer wet

maintenance.wateringNotes: No supplemental irrigation once established; first-year plants accept occasional March soak only.
maintenance.pruningTask: Lightly tip-prune rangy stems after yellow flower peak
maintenance.pruningNotes: Avoid cutting to bare wood; light summer shaping encourages bushy resprout from latent buds.
maintenance.specialNotes: Host to native bruchid beetles—interpret seed damage as food-web richness, not failure.

seed.description: Twisted legume pods split explosively—rodents and quail cache seeds in scrub openings

phenology.ecologicalValue: Pea flowers feed early bumblebees; foliage supports blue butterfly larvae that link legumes to aerial insectivores.

wildlife.notes: Larvae feed on fresh legume foliage and partner with attendant ants on tender shoots.
Works small yellow papilionaceous flowers along low mounding stems.
Buzzes pea flowers to release pollen onto ventral scopa.
Gleans small legume seeds once pods twist open in early summer.

description: Spring annual that carpets disturbed sandy soils with nitrogen-rich tissue and pea-flowers, feeding pollen-specialist bees and lycaenid larvae whose populations ripple up to swallows and flycatchers in Tallamy’s seasonal pulse of insect biomass.

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Sandy flats, road cuts, and gentle slopes where winter rains percolate quickly
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Deep sand to sandy loam; intolerant of heavy clay that stays saturated

maintenance.wateringNotes: Sow with winter rains; irrigated gardens should mimic natural wet-dry cycle—deep soak weekly February–April, then dry down as pods mature.
maintenance.pruningTask: Pull or cut spent plants after pods split to reduce disease carryover
maintenance.pruningNotes: Shake ripe pods over bare soil if you want a denser stand next spring.
maintenance.specialNotes: Fixes nitrogen—pair with late-emerging perennials that benefit from enriched root zones.

seed.description: Hairy legume pods split to release mottled seeds that persist in soil seed banks

phenology.ecologicalValue: Dense racemes feed early bumblebee queens; foliage hosts lycaenid larvae tied to ant mutualisms.

wildlife.notes: Larvae use lupine foliage and associate with nectar-foraging ants on growing tips.
Migrants tank up on pea-family nectar before continuing north or east.
Queens and workers mob upright racemes during cool spring mornings.
Strips drying legume pods for protein-rich seeds before plants senesce.
